Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd sit down with good intentions but then find myself just staring at the screen or getting distracted. With 19 online courses, everything kind of blurred together and I never established a consistent system. I'd study in bursts, usually when panic set in about something being due. The anxiety made it hard to focus even when I tried to organize myself.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, driven entirely by deadlines and whatever crisis was happening that week. Some weeks I'd put in serious hours, other weeks almost nothing. I wanted to be more consistent but the stress and anxiety made it hard to stick to any schedule. When you're managing that many courses and feeling overwhelmed, regularity just doesn't happen naturally.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or realizing I'd missed something important. Sometimes I'd get an email notification that would remind me to check in. I rarely logged in just to browse or stay on top of things proactively. It was more reactive than anything else, which I know isn't ideal but that's how it went.
Q4: Not really, no. I never got to the point where checking the LMS felt automatic or natural. It always felt like a chore I was avoiding. Maybe for a week or two I'd try to make it habitual, but then life would get chaotic and I'd fall back into only logging in when absolutely necessary.
Q5: The videos were probably the most useful because I learn better by watching and doing rather than reading. I watched quite a bit of video content when I could focus. Discussion posts sometimes helped me understand what other students were thinking, which was useful when I was confused about concepts. But honestly, even the useful stuff felt overwhelming given everything I was juggling.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the supplementary readings and optional materials. With my anxiety levels, I had to triage constantly, so anything that wasn't directly going to affect my grade got pushed aside. Some resources just seemed too dense or time-consuming when I was already struggling to complete the basics. I told myself I'd get to them later but never did.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate connection to passing the course or if it was hands-on and practical. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so anything I could actually do rather than just read about felt more valuable. If something seemed abstract or disconnected from assignments, I'd lose interest quickly.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're barely keeping up with required work and your anxiety is through the roof, optional stuff just adds to the guilt pile. Required activities had urgency attached to them, which was sometimes the only thing that got me moving. Optional ones just made me feel worse about not doing them.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture slides or PDFs and study from those on my phone or printed copies. Sometimes I'd work through problems on paper without touching the computer at all. I also used YouTube videos and other external sites when the course materials weren't clicking for me. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours working through practice problems in a notebook or rewatching downloaded videos, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd also study with a classmate in person, talking through concepts, which was really helpful but completely invisible online. The LMS only sees clicks, not the actual thinking and processing happening offline.
Q11: All the time. I'd open a video or document once, download it or take screenshots, and then never access it through the LMS again. My internet isn't always reliable, so having offline copies made sense. Plus, I could study on the bus or wherever without worrying about connectivity. The LMS would show one view but I might have spent hours with that material.
Q12: Around midterms and when multiple deadlines piled up, I just hit a wall. The anxiety got so bad I could barely function, and I'd freeze up instead of working. There were also personal things happening that drained my energy. When you're already running on empty and then get surprised by an assignment you forgot about, it's hard to bounce back.
Q13: Absolutely. The workload across all my courses was crushing, and I was constantly confused about priorities. Fatigue from stress and poor sleep made everything harder. Some days I'd log into the LMS, see everything I needed to do, and just log right back out because it was too overwhelming to even start.
Q14: Many times. I'd be scrolling through materials but my brain was somewhere else entirely, worrying about other things or just zoned out from exhaustion. I'd watch videos without really absorbing anything, then realize I needed to rewatch them. Being physically present in the LMS didn't mean I was actually learning.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when they felt too big or confusing. The anxiety made starting really difficult because I'd catastrophize about doing it wrong. I knew certain assignments were important but the fear of failure made me avoid them until the last possible moment. It's a terrible cycle but hard to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd have a burst of activity right before something was due, cramming everything in at once. Without a deadline, I'd drift and procrastinate indefinitely. The structure of due dates was both stressful and necessary for me to function at all.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss. When I got encouraging feedback, it gave me a brief boost, but negative feedback sometimes made my anxiety worse and I'd avoid that course for a while. I wanted feedback to help me improve, but emotionally it was hard to receive sometimes. It didn't consistently change my engagement patterns.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so much content spread across the semester. I needed more scaffolding and check-ins to stay on track, but things felt very self-directed. When you have low resilience like me, a loose structure means you can easily fall behind without anyone noticing until it's too late.
Q19: Having everything online made it easier to access but also easier to avoid. The lack of face-to-face accountability meant I could disappear for a while without consequences. Clearer weekly checklists or more interactive elements might have helped me stay engaged. The design assumed a level of self-regulation I didn't have.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually completing work. You might log in multiple times to look at an assignment, stress about it, and leave without submitting. High activity doesn't mean productive activity when you're paralyzed by anxiety or confusion about what to do.
Q21: That sounds familiar actually. I didn't log in much but I did pass my courses. A lot of my studying happened offline with downloaded materials or external resources. Someone might look disengaged online but be putting in real work elsewhere. The LMS doesn't capture everything that contributes to learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you click on, but they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually understanding material or just scrolling mindlessly. They also miss all the offline work, the emotional state you're in, and whether you're genuinely learning or just going through motions.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious and want to know more, not just checking boxes to pass. For me, real engagement has been rare this semester because anxiety and stress made everything feel like survival mode rather than genuine learning.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected everything, how many times I started something and had to stop because I was overwhelmed. It wouldn't show the offline studying, the mental health struggles, or the surprise I felt when things went differently than expected. It captures clicks, not the human experience of trying to learn while barely holding it together.
